comment: A factual accident report noting the victims were Tabligh Jamaat members traveling to a mosque. The article is purely descriptive of the traffic accident and victim identification, with no editorializing about the organization itself.

An explanatory/educational piece describing the 'six usul' principles of Tabligh Jamaat, sourced from an academic book. The tone is descriptive and informational, presenting the group's core tenets as doctrine rather than journalistic praise or criticism.

This is a devotional, first-person opinion piece by an imam association official celebrating Tabligh Jamaat's history and global mission in reverent religious language, including an explicit prayer ('may Allah keep this effort ongoing till the Day of Judgment'). The framing is one of glorification and moral elevation rather than neutral reporting.

The article reports factionalism within Tabligh Jamaat (Sa'd vs. Zubair followers) leading to violence between groups and the confinement/intimidation of a college principal. While quotes are attributed to various parties, the narrative centers on disruptive, coercive behavior stemming from internal Tabligh disputes, giving an unfavorable cast to the organization's internal conflict, even though the reporting itself remains largely factual and procedural.

A procedural report on university students proposing government mediation for the Sa'd-Zubair Tabligh Jamaat dispute. Although the students' statement frames the internal conflict as a 'threat to stability,' this is presented as a quoted position, not the reporter's own framing; the article remains a neutral account of a press conference and proposals.

A laudatory, reverent essay on Tabligh Jamaat founder Muhammad Ilyas Kandhlawi, describing him as a revered Islamic scholar and saint ('shaykh'), extensively praising his teachings and legacy, and framing his malfuzat as an invaluable spiritual document for the Muslim world today. This is clear glorification rather than neutral reporting.

A local report on a Tabligh Markaz mosque opening in a defunct cinema hall, including a quote from a local businessman expressing happiness that the building now serves 'a good purpose.' The coverage is factual and mildly appreciative of the community outcome but does not editorialize about Tabligh Jamaat as an organization.

This is the same explanatory piece on the six usul principles as another article, with an added first-person researcher anecdote describing personal experience within the group. The tone remains descriptive/informational about religious practice rather than evaluative journalism.

An analytical piece examining sociological reasons behind Tabligh Jamaat's growing global popularity (apolitical stance, afterlife focus, role in Western mosque-building). The tone is explanatory and academic, noting both growth and recent internal division without strong evaluative language toward the organization.

A purely descriptive, encyclopedic account of Tabligh Jamaat's various proselytizing practices (chilla, mastura jamaat, etc.), sourced from an academic reference. It presents organizational procedures factually without positive or negative framing.
